title: 2D kinematic analysis of walk, trot, and canter gaits in Criollo breed horse
abstract: This study described the linear, temporal, and angular kinematic characteristics of thirty-five Criollo horses in walk, trot, and canter using 2D video motion capture and analysis. Retroreflective markers were placed on anatomical landmarks of the fore- and hindlimbs, and kinematic variables assessed with a motion analysis software. In walk, a mean stride length of 1.61 ± 0.14 m, stride duration of 0.90 ± 0.04 s, and mean velocity of 1.79 ± 0.13 m/s were observed. In trot, the mean stride length was 2.30 ± 0.23 m, stride duration was 0.60 ± 0.03 s, and the mean velocity was 3.82 ± 0.43 m/s. In canter, the stride length, stride duration, and velocity values were measured for the lead (2.86 ± 0.29 m, 0.51 ± 0.03 s, and 5.61 ± 0.64 m/s) and trail limbs (2.68 ± 0.25 m, 0.51 ± 0.02 s, and 5.28 ± 0.56 m/s). Regarding the angular variables, during the walk, the Criollo horses reached maximum protraction and retraction angles of the forelimbs of 16.90 ± 2.31º and 22.99 ± 3.47º, respectively. For the hindlimbs, the maximum protraction was 10.86 ± 2.45º, while the retraction was 31.47 ± 2.13º. The joints numerically showed smaller ranges of motion (ROM) during the canter, especially in the shoulder, elbow, and hip joints. The values increased for walk and trot and decreased again for the canter, indicating a relationship between gait velocity and joint ROM. These results provided reference kinematic values for Criollo horses; further studies are needed to evaluate specific training or management applications.
text:
2D Kinematic analysis of walk, trot, and canter gaits in Criollo breed horses.
Ciência Rural, v.56, n.3, 2026.
1
2D Kinematic analysis of walk, trot, and canter gaits in Criollo breed horses
Análise  cinemática  2D  do  passo,  trote  e  canter  de  cavalos  da  raça  Crioula
Gino  Luigi  Bonilla  Lemos  Pizzi1    Karina  Holz2    Éverton  Augusto  Kowalski2
Priscila  Fonseca  Ribeiro2    Roberta  Blake3*    Charles  Ferreira  Martins1

INTRODUCTION
The equine species encompasses a wide 
variety of breeds and biotypes, with conformational, 
morphometric, and biomechanical characteristics 
generally associated with breed selection standards 
(BEEMAN, 2008). In this context, the Criollo 
breed emerged as a central figure in Latin American 
equestrian sports by the late 20th century, solidifying 
its role as a cultural and economic pillar across 
southern Brazil, Argentina, Chile, and Uruguay. This 
breed is deeply embedded in local culture due to the 
rural lifestyle heritage in these regions (KARLAU 
et al., 2023; VINOCUR et al., 2003). The rising 
popularity of the breed has driven the evolution 
of competitions aiming to reward horses that best 
combine morphological and functional traits within 
the category. 
1Departamento de Clínicas Veterinárias, Faculdade de Veterinária, Universidade Federal de Pelotas (UFPel), Capão do Leão, RS, Brasil.
2Programa de Pós-graduação em Zootecnia, Faculdade de Agronomia Eliseu Maciel, Universidade Federal de Pelotas (UFPel), Capão do Leão, 
RS, Brasil.
3Anglia Ruskin University, Lordship Road, Chelmsford, CM1 3RR, United Kingdom. E-mail: roberta.blake@aru.ac.uk. *Corresponding author.
ABSTRACT: This study described the linear, temporal, and angular kinematic characteristics of thirty-five Criollo horses in walk, trot, and 
canter using 2D video motion capture and analysis. Retroreflective markers were placed on anatomical landmarks of the fore- and hindlimbs, 
and kinematic variables assessed with a motion analysis software. In walk, a mean stride length of 1.61 ± 0.14 m, stride duration of 0.90 ± 0.04 
s, and mean velocity of 1.79 ± 0.13 m/s were observed. In trot, the mean stride length was 2.30 ± 0.23 m, stride duration was 0.60 ± 0.03 s, 
and the mean velocity was 3.82 ± 0.43 m/s. In canter, the stride length, stride duration, and velocity values were measured for the lead (2.86 ± 
0.29 m, 0.51 ± 0.03 s, and 5.61 ± 0.64 m/s) and trail limbs (2.68 ± 0.25 m, 0.51 ± 0.02 s, and 5.28 ± 0.56 m/s). Regarding the angular variables, 
during the walk, the Criollo horses reached maximum protraction and retraction angles of the forelimbs of 16.90 ± 2.31º and 22.99 ± 3.47º, 
respectively. For the hindlimbs, the maximum protraction was 10.86 ± 2.45º, while the retraction was 31.47 ± 2.13º. The joints numerically 
showed smaller ranges of motion (ROM) during the canter, especially in the shoulder, elbow, and hip joints. The values increased for walk 
and trot and decreased again for the canter, indicating a relationship between gait velocity and joint ROM. These results provided reference 
kinematic values for Criollo horses; further studies are needed to evaluate specific training or management applications.
Key words: equine biomechanics, gaits, musculoskeletal system, orthopaedics, kinesiology.
RESUMO: O objetivo principal deste estudo foi descrever as características cinemáticas lineares, temporais e angulares dos membros 
torácicos e posteriores de cavalos Crioulos no passo, trote e galope utilizando captura e análise de movimento em vídeo 2D. Trinta e cinco 
cavalos Crioulos clinicamente saudáveis (5 - 10 anos) foram filmados em condições controladas com um sistema de vídeo 2D a 240 fps. 
Marcadores retroreflexivos foram posicionados em pontos anatômicos dos membros torácico e pélvico, e as variáveis cinemáticas foram 
extraídas utilizando o software Quintic Biomechanics® v33. No passo, foram observados comprimento médio da passada de 1,61 ± 0,14 m, 
duração da passada de 0,90 ± 0,04 s e velocidade média de 1,79 ± 0,13 m/s. No trote, o comprimento médio da passada foi de 2,30 ± 0,23 m, a 
duração da passada foi de 0,60 ± 0,03  s e a velocidade média foi de 3,82 ± 0,43 m/s. No galope, os valores de comprimento da passada, duração 
da passada e velocidade para a guia (2,86 ± 0,29 m, 0,51 ± 0,03 s e 5,61 ± 0,64 m/s) e membros da trilha (2,68 ± 0,25 m, 0,51 ± 0,02 s e 5,28 
± 0,56 m/s). Em relação às variáveis angulares, durante o passo, os cavalos Crioulos atingiram ângulos máximos de protração e retração dos 
membros torácicos de 16,90 ± 2,31º e 22,99 ± 3,47º, respectivamente. Para os membros posteriores, a protração máxima foi de 10,86 ± 2,45º, 
enquanto a retração foi de 31,47 ± 2,13º. As articulações apresentaram numericamente menores amplitudes de movimento (ADM) durante 
o galope, principalmente nas articulações do ombro, cotovelo e quadril. Os valores aumentaram para passo e trote e diminuíram novamente 
para galope, indicando relação entre velocidade de marcha e ADM articular. Estes resultados fornecem valores cinemáticos de referência para 
cavalos Crioulos; estudos adicionais são necessários para avaliar aplicações específicas em treinamento ou manejo.
Palavras-chave: biomecânica equina, andaduras, sistema musculoesquelético, clínica ortopédica, cinesiologia.
CLINIC AND SURGERY
2
Ciência Rural, v.56, n.3, 2026.
Pizzi et al.
The 
Criollo 
horse 
excels 
in 
both 
competitive equestrian events, such as the Freio de 
Ouro, where its agility and balance are demonstrated 
in manoeuvres like the esbarrada and volta sobre 
patas, and in practical fieldwork, particularly cattle 
handling, showcasing the breed’s versatility and 
genetic refinement for both sport and ranch tasks 
across South America (PIZZI et al., 2024). The Freio 
de Ouro, the primary event for the morpho-functional 
selection of Criollo horses, is a rigorous competition 
held in Brazil that tests and highlights these animals’ 
physical and psychological preparation, muscular 
strength, and endurance (CUCCO et al., 2016). 
This prestigious event serves as both a competitive 
showcase and a breeding evaluation, where only the 
finest horses advance in the breed registry, contributing 
to the selective improvement of Criollo lines. In 
2022, genealogical records from the Associação 
Brasileira dos Criadores de Cavalos da Raça Crioula 
(ABCCC) – the main registry association for the 
breed – reported a remarkable 39.42% increase in 
registered animals, surpassing 460,000, the majority 
of which are concentrated in southern Brazil.
Conformational and performance traits are 
hereditary and influenced by environmental factors. 
Although the conformation of the Criollo breed has 
been studied by various authors, primarily based 
on body measurements and indices (DALTO, 2006; 
PIMENTEL et al., 2018a; PIMENTAL et al., 2018b), 
comprehensive studies on the gaits performed by this 
breed and their morphological influences are lacking. 
Although, linear and angular measurements of the 
limbs and joints, whether assessed statically or during 
motion, can help identify the types of movements 
horses perform, a complete 2D kinematic analysis 
can provide a quantitative analysis of each gait. 
Computerised kinematic analyses allow the examiner 
to record and assess the movements of the individual 
during one or more stride cycles (SANDBERG 
et al., 2020). This data can describe the geometry 
of movement in terms of displacement, velocity, 
and acceleration, facilitating the evaluation of joint 
angles, angular velocities, temporal data, and stride 
length (SUTHERLAND, 2002).
Numerous horse breeds have had their 
gait patterns characterized, but a comprehensive 
study has never been conducted for the Criollo breed 
(ROBILLIARD et al., 2007). Despite the breed’s 
prevalence in South America, it remains relatively 
unknown within the global veterinary scientific 
community, particularly in terms of biomechanical 
studies. Previous studies have addressed the 
morphometric and biomechanical aspects of Criollo 
horses (PAGANELA et al., 2012; PIMENTEL et 
al., 2018a; PIMENTEL et al., 2018b; VERNA et 
al., 2022a); however, they are scarce and do not 
encompass a complete kinematic analysis of the 
primary gaits these equines perform at a competitive 
level. Within this context, a series of investigations 
into the kinematic parameters of the breed was 
established to gather extensive information on all 
the different gaits performed by these individuals. 
Therefore, the primary objective of this study 
described the quantitative kinematic variables – 
linear, temporal, and angular – of the thoracic and 
pelvic limbs of Criollo horses in walk, trot, and canter.
MATERIALS   AND   METHODS
Study design
All procedures performed were approved 
by the Animal Ethics Committee (CEUA) of the 
Universidade Federal de Pelotas (UFPel), under 
registration number 51839-2019. The processing and 
analysis of the collected videos were conducted at the 
School of Equine and Veterinary Physiotherapy, part 
of Anglia Ruskin University, in the United Kingdom.
Experimental design
Thirty-five Criollo breed horses (Equus 
caballus) were evaluated, duly registered with the 
ABCCC. The horses were aged between 5 and 10 
years, comprising 23 males and 12 females, with a 
mean weight of 428.81 ± 24.09 kg and a mean withers 
height of 1.42 ± 0.02 m. These horses originated from 
10 training centres in the micro-region of Pelotas, 
in southern Rio Grande do Sul, Brazil. All selected 
horses were trained competitors in the Freio de 
Ouro discipline. The discipline requires horses to 
perform specific gaits and manoeuvres that showcase 
their agility and skill in handling cattle, replicating 
the demands of fieldwork. The equestrian surfaces 
were carefully controlled to match the conditions 
of the Freio de Ouro qualifying stages. The training 
routine for these horses consisted of a minimum two-
year period involving a weekly exercise regimen, 
lasting around one hour per day. This regimen 
alternated between 10-minute walks and trots, as 
well as 10-minute canter exercises, followed by cool-
down walks to stabilize heart rates to physiological 
limits. Each training centre employed its own rider 
responsible for the training, following similar 
routines at least five times per week. The competition 
regulations permit animals of any age and gender, 
with an equal number of male and female horses 
qualifying for the final stage.
2D Kinematic analysis of walk, trot, and canter gaits in Criollo breed horses.
Ciência Rural, v.56, n.3, 2026.
3
Data collection was carried out at 
equestrian training centres in June 2022. All animals 
were subjected to the same predefined experimental 
and environmental conditions. Prior to kinematic 
analysis, the horses underwent a specific clinical 
examination of the locomotor system to determine the 
health status of the sample population. An experienced 
clinician conducted the evaluation and determined a 
grade of 0 on the American Association of Equine 
Practitioners (AAEP) lameness scale, indicating no 
lameness in the tested animals. All individuals were 
considered sound, with no presence of lameness or 
other musculoskeletal disorders.
The 
biotype 
of 
the 
sample 
was 
homogeneous and consistent with the phenotypic 
standards of the species, as the horses from the 
equestrian training centres belong to the Criollo 
breed and meet the category’s requirements. Gender 
was not considered a factor as there are no significant 
morphometric differences between genders in this 
breed (PIMENTEL et al., 2018a).
Kinematic data collection
The positioning of markers and the setup 
of the study field were based on the methodology 
outlined by TORRES-PÉREZ et al. (2017). Twenty-
four semi-spherical retroreflective markers (30mm in 
diameter) were positioned and affixed with double-
sided tape by the same operator on the right and 
left sides of the animals (Figure 1). The anatomical 
locations 
included 
the 
prominent 
anatomical 
landmarks of each limb: spine of the scapula, greater 
tubercle of humerus, lateral humeral epicondyle, 
ulnar carpal bone, lateral metacarpal epicondyle, 
distal interphalangeal joint, for the forelimbs and 
ventral aspect of the tuber coxae, great trochanter of 
the femur, lateral femoral epicondyle, lateral tubercle 
of the talus, lateral metatarsal epicondyle, and distal 
interphalangeal joint for the pelvic limbs.
The study area measured 10 metres in 
length and 3 metres in width, marked by cones for easy 
identification. Lateral space allowed for deceleration 
and repositioning of the animals, maintaining constant 
velocity and gait pattern, preventing transitions. A 
high-velocity camera (240fps, resolution 1280x550) 
was levelled horizontally on a fixed tripod 1 metre in 
height, positioned 10 metres from the centre of the 
platform. A 72W LED light was placed above the 
camera to activate the reflectivity of the markers. A 
1-metre ruler was placed at the centre of the field, both 
horizontally and vertically, for system calibration. 
This setup was standardized for all training centres 
and repeated uniformly at each location. For this 
study, the horses were ridden. To guarantee the 
Figure 1 - Placement of retroreflective markers on thoracic and pelvic limbs of Criollo 
horses for kinematic analysis and angle measured during the analysis (1) 
spine of the scapula, (2) greater tubercle of humerus, (3) lateral humeral 
epicondyle, (4) ulnar carpal bone, (5) lateral metacarpal epicondyle, (6) distal 
interphalangeal joint, (7) ventral aspect of the tuber coxae, (8) Great trochanter 
of the femur, (9) Lateral femoral epicondyle, (10) Lateral tubercle of the talus, 
(11) Lateral metatarsal epicondyle, (12) distal interphalangeal joint.
4
Ciência Rural, v.56, n.3, 2026.
Pizzi et al.
accuracy of the kinematic analysis, each horse was 
equipped with its own tack, which was positioned 
to minimise any potential interference with the 
placement of the retroreflective markers. These 
markers were meticulously positioned to prevent any 
obstruction from the equipment, thereby ensuring 
precise measurement of the horses’ movements.
Prior to data collection, the horses 
underwent a 10-minute warm-up at walk and trot. 
Subsequently, riders guided the animals in a straight 
line to the centre of the study field. Three slow-
motion videos, each 10 seconds long, were recorded 
for each gait (walk, trot, and canter) from both sides 
for each horse, simulating the gaits performed during 
official competition trials. To ensure consistency in 
velocity across trials, each horse was conducted in 
a straight line to the centre of the study field, where 
they executed the gaits under controlled conditions 
that closely mimicked competition-level execution, 
thereby allowing for a reliable assessment of kinematic 
variables. After collection, the videos were processed 
and analyzed using the Quintic Biomechanics® v33 
2D motion analysis system, where the obtained 
variables were processed and quantified.
Kinematic variables analysis
The analyzed variables were divided into 
temporal, linear, and angular categories and were 
measured individually for the thoracic and pelvic limbs 
from videos of both sides. Specifically, in the canter, it 
was also analyzed whether the filmed side was the lead 
or trail limb, based on the last thoracic limb to touch 
the ground during a complete stride cycle. Initially, 
all videos were measured in terms of stride length and 
duration, based on the first frame in which the thoracic 
limb touches the ground until the contact occurs again, 
completing the cycle of a full stride. Following this, 
the stride length-to-duration ratio was calculated 
to determine the velocity of each gait. Specifically, 
velocity was computed by dividing the measured stride 
length (the distance covered in one complete stride) by 
the corresponding duration (the time taken to complete 
that stride). Subsequently, within the group of temporal 
variables, the stance time(s), suspension time(s), and 
duty factor (examined as the ratio of stance time to the 
total stride time) were examined.
Finally, the angular variables investigated 
were measured following the methodology described 
by MORALES et al. (1998) and included the 
protraction angle (º), retraction angle (º), and the 
maximum fetlock extension angle during mid-stance 
(º), which is the angle where the respective limb 
had the fetlock perpendicular to the camera at the 
intermediate point of the stance phase. Additionally, 
a tracking protocol was implemented, involving 
real-time angular mapping of the thoracic and pelvic 
limb joints throughout a complete stride cycle. This 
yielded minimum angles (flexion), maximum angles 
(extension), and ROM (range of motion), defined as 
the difference between the two, for the shoulder, elbow, 
carpus, forelimb fetlock, and proximal interphalangeal 
joints (thoracic limbs), and the hip, stifle, hock, 
hindlimb fetlock, and proximal interphalangeal joints 
(pelvic limbs). All analyses considered frames where 
the animal was closest to the centre of the study field to 
avoid perspective and parallax errors.
Statistical analysis
The three gaits were described according 
to the quantitative values of each kinematic variable. 
For this purpose, the mean values from the three 
collected videos of each side in walk, trot, and 
canter were considered; and subsequently, the mean 
value between the right and left sides was adopted. 
Descriptive statistical analyses were performed using 
IBM® SPSS® v29. Descriptive values of the final 
means and standard deviations (SD) of all linear, 
temporal, and angular kinematic variables were 
obtained for each thoracic (right: RF; left: LF) and 
pelvic limb (right: RH; left: LH).
RESULTS
A total of 18 videos were processed for 
each horse across three distinct gaits, equalling 
to a total of 630 strides per limb and 3,150 strides 
analysed. Gaits have been identified and classified 
according to ROBILLIARD et al. (2007).
Linear kinematic characteristics
The walk achieved a mean stride length 
of 1.61 ± 0.14 m, stride duration of 0.90 ± 0.04 s, 
and velocity of 1.79 ± 0.13 m/s. For the trot, the 
values found were: stride length 2.30 ± 0.23 m, stride 
duration 0.60 ± 0.03 s, and velocity 3.82 ± 0.43 m/s. 
In the canter, 54.44% of the videos showed animals 
executing a right lead canter (with the right forelimb 
being the last thoracic limb to touch the ground), and 
the stride length, stride duration, and velocity were 
measured for the lead limbs (2.86 ± 0.29 m, 0.51 ± 
0.03 s, and 5.61 ± 0.64 m/s) and the trail limbs (2.68 
± 0.25 m, 0.51 ± 0.02 s, and 5.28 ± 0.56 m/s). 
Temporal kinematic variables
The stance time of the thoracic limbs of 
Criollo horses during walking was 0.49 ± 0.03 s, and 
2D Kinematic analysis of walk, trot, and canter gaits in Criollo breed horses.
Ciência Rural, v.56, n.3, 2026.
5
for the pelvic limbs, it was 0.50 ± 0.04 s, while the 
suspension time was 0.35 ± 0.03 s and 0.33 ± 0.03 s 
for the respective limbs. The mean duty factor for the 
thoracic limbs was 0.54 ± 0.02 and 0.55 ± 0.03 for 
the pelvic limbs. In the trot, these values were 0.22 
± 0.03s for both thoracic and pelvic limbs for stance 
time, and the swing time was 0.32 ± 0.03 s for the 
thoracic limbs and 0.33 ± 0.02 s for the pelvic limbs, 
with a mean duty factor of 0.35 ±0.03 (thoracic) and 
0.35 ± 0.04 (pelvic). Regarding the canter, both the 
lead thoracic and pelvic limbs showed a stance time 
of 0.15 ± 0.02 s, while the trail limb showed a stance 
time of 0.14 ± 0.02 s. The suspension time of the lead 
limbs (thoracic and pelvic) in this gait was 0.29 ± 
0.03 s and 0.29 ± 0.02, respectively. The values for 
this variable were 0.29 ± 0.02 s for the trail forelimb 
and 0.30 ± 0.03 s for the trail hindlimb. Finally, in 
the canter, the mean duty factor for the lead thoracic 
limb was 0.29 ± 0.03, for the trail limb 0.28 ± 0.03, 
for the lead pelvic limb 0.30 ± 0.04, and for the 
trail pelvic limb 0.28 ± 0.04. Stance time was the 
variable that showed the greatest numerical variation 
between gaits compared to suspension time, with 
both decreasing as velocity increased. The detailed 
values of the temporal variables by limb in each gait 
are presented in table 1.
Angular kinematic variables
In terms of angular variables, during the 
walk, Criollo horses reached a maximum protraction 
angle of 16.90 ± 2.31º and a retraction angle of 
22.99 ± 3.47º for the thoracic limbs, with a mean 
hyperextension angle of 228.22 ± 5.48º at the fetlock 
during mid-stance. For the pelvic limbs, the maximum 
protraction was 10.86 ± 2.45º, while the retraction 
was 31.47 ± 2.13º, and the hyperextension angle at 
the fetlock was 214.63 ± 5.77º. Regarding the trot, the 
protraction angles for the thoracic limbs were 16.26 
± 3.10º and the retraction angles were 20.84 ± 2.77º, 
with a maximum hyperextension angle at the fetlock 
of 239.6 ± 6.40º during stance. For the pelvic limbs, 
the maximum protraction and retraction angles, as 
well as the hyperextension angle at the fetlock during 
mid-stance, were 10.35 ± 2.04º, 30.12 ± 1.91º, and 
234.88 ± 9.98º, respectively. Table 2 shows detailed 
results of the angular variables for each thoracic and 
pelvic limb during the walk and trot, as well as the 
discriminated values for lead and trail limbs during 
the canter. The protraction and retraction angles were 
numerically similar between the thoracic and pelvic 
limbs with the change in velocity from walk to trot. 
These variables’ values were higher in the canter for 
the lead limb, with greater protraction in the thoracic 
limbs and lower in the pelvic limbs compared to 
the trail limb, while the retraction angle was greater 
in both ipsilateral lead limbs. The mean trail angle 
increased numerically with the increase in velocity 
and with the change in gait in all limbs.
The shoulder joint of Criollo horses 
showed the smallest ROM in both the walk and trot, 
with mean values of 5.71 ± 2.11º and 6.25 ± 3.30º, 
respectively. At the elbow joint, the amplitude was 
greater in the walk, with an angle of 48.33 ± 6.36º, 
slightly decreasing in the trot to 46.60 ± 9.81°. In the 
carpal region, the ROM during the walk was 64.44 ± 
5.66º, increasing to 73.50 ± 7.65º during the trot. The 
range of motion at the thoracic fetlock also showed 
an increase from the walk (62.49 ± 9.93º) to the trot

Pizzi et al.
(66.85 ± 10.70º). Regarding the pelvic limbs, the hip 
joint had a ROM of 16.71 ± 3.46º during the walk, 
decreasing to 14.45 ± 5.66 in the trot. The mean 
ROM values for the stifle joint were 26.24 ± 4.07º 
for the walk and 25.47 ± 4.64º for the trot, showing 
similar numbers in both gaits. In the tarsal region, 
individuals showed greater amplitude, with a ROM 
of 36.14 ± 6.01º during the walk, increasing to 42.19 
± 7.15º in the trot. However, at the level of the pelvic 
fetlock, the highest amplitude values were observed 
in both the walk and trot, being 72.46 ± 9.10º and 
79.22 ± 8.69º, respectively. The ROM of all joints 
in the thoracic and pelvic segments showed smaller 
amplitudes during the canter for both lead and trail 
limbs. The maximum extension and flexion angles of 
the thoracic and pelvic limbs (walk and trot) and lead 
and trail limbs in the canter, as well as the ROM for 
each joint, are shown in table 3 and table 4.
DISCUSSION
This study provided a comprehensive 
kinematic analysis of the Criollo horse breed, focusing 
on key gait characteristics at walk, trot, and canter. The 
results established reference ranges for stride length, 
stride duration, and angular variables that distinguish 
the Criollo gait and offer a robust foundation for 
further biomechanical studies. These findings serve 
as a baseline for assessing gait conformity to breed 
standards and understanding the breed’s unique 
adaptations to various equestrian tasks, particularly 
those requiring endurance and agility.
The kinematic parameters observed in the 
Criollo horses reveal stride and angular characteristics 
that correspond well with their functional morphology, 
favouring sustained movement over high-speed 
exertion. This study identified marked differences in 
stride length and duration correlated with increasing 
velocity, in line with previous findings suggesting 
that higher velocities demand longer stride lengths 
and shorter stride durations to optimise locomotion 
efficiency (BACK & CLAYTON, 2013). Criollo 
horses’ morphological traits – medium to short stature 
(1.40 to 1.55 m), compact muscular bodies, and 
robust limbs – are naturally adapted for endurance-
oriented gaits rather than rapid bursts, contrasting 
with breeds bred for explosive or sustained high-
speed events, such as the Quarter Horse and the 
Thoroughbred 
(VERGARA-HERNANDEZ 
et 
al., 2022). Specifically, Quarter Horses exhibit a 
shorter, muscular build well-suited for high-speed, 
short-duration events, while Thoroughbreds, with 
their leaner and taller physiques, are tailored for 
extended high-velocity performance. Criollo horses’

2D Kinematic analysis of walk, trot, and canter gaits in Criollo breed horses.
Ciência Rural, v.56, n.3, 2026.
7
gait parameters thus reflect a pattern that prioritises 
endurance, agility, and versatility, aligning well with 
the demands of equestrian competitions like the Freio 
de Ouro, where balance between stamina and agility 
is essential.
Furthermore, the stride characteristics 
documented in this study provide an effective baseline 
for breed-specific comparisons. For example, Quarter 
Horses and Thoroughbreds have been shown to 
achieve stride lengths of 5.90 ± 0.11 m and 4.40 ± 0.11 
m, respectively, at gallop (VERGARA-HERNANDEZ 
et al., 2022), whereas Criollo horses demonstrated 
stride values more consistent with slower, sustained 
gaits. Rather than comparing to gallop data – since 
canter and gallop are biomechanically different gaits 
– this study focuses on the kinematic values observed 
in Criollo horses. Our results showed a mean stride 
length of 1.61 ± 0.14 m at the walk, which rises to 2.30 
± 0.23 m at the trot and further to 2.86 ± 0.29 m at 
the canter. These increasing values reflect the expected 
progression of stride extension with gait speed and 
provide specific reference values for future intra-breed 
or matched-gait comparisons. As expected, velocity 
increased proportionally with each gait transition (e.g., 
from 1.79 ± 0.13 m/s at walk to 5.61 ± 0.64 m/s at 
canter), which corresponds with similar kinematic 
changes documented in other breeds (CLAYTON, 
1994a; CLAYTON, 1994b).
Recent literature underscores that as horses’ 
speed increases, so too do their stride frequency and 
limb angulation, with biomechanical adaptations to 
accommodate joint loading and muscle activation 
demands (CLARK et al., 2020; PUGLIESE et al., 2020). 
These adaptations reflect compensatory responses 
necessary for supporting efficient motion at higher 
velocities, as evidenced in racing breeds (BARREY, 
2013). However, it is worth noting that while Criollo 
horses’ kinematic profiles reveal characteristics suited 
to their endurance-based activities, extrapolations 
regarding injury risk or specific training protocols 
should be treated cautiously, as they exceed the scope 
of this study’s findings.

8
Ciência Rural, v.56, n.3, 2026.
Pizzi et al.
Criollo horses, noted for their versatility 
across equestrian disciplines, demonstrated kinematic 
adaptability, particularly in trot and canter gaits, akin 
to those observed in dressage and reining horses. 
This versatility is a critical asset in the Freio de Ouro 
competition, which assesses manoeuvrability and 
comfort, especially in cattle tasks. The trot and canter 
velocities documented here (mean trot: 3.61 ± 0.10 
m/s, canter: 3.91 ± 0.17 m/s) align with previous 
dressage studies (CLAYTON, 1994a; CLAYTON, 
1994b), supporting the functional adaptability of 
the Criollo gait. However, breed classification 
relies on specific locomotion patterns, as noted by 
ROBILLIARD et al. (2007). 
Across the three primary gaits – walk, 
trot, and canter – stance and swing times were 
similar, indicating balanced temporal dynamics 
with minimal asymmetry. In Criollo horses, the 
near-identical stance and swing times at walk, trot, 
and canter not only correspond with the gait-speed 
– dependent saddle pressure patterns that centralize 
load under the rider’s saddle, but also exploit elastic 
strain energy storage in distal limb tendons to reduce 
active muscular work by up to 80%, and align with 
the third metacarpal’s nonuniform strain energy 
density distributions that protect bone integrity under 
varying load conditions (FRUEHWIRTH et al., 
2004; HARRISON et al., 2010; RUBIN et al., 2013). 
Comparative studies have shown that while general 
temporal trends appear across breeds, Criollo-specific 
values reflect the breed’s unique conformation and 
biomechanics (ROBILLIARD et al., 2007). Further 
research could explore how training interventions 
affect temporal parameters, with longitudinal studies 
offering insights into gait dynamics over time. By 
establishing these kinematic benchmarks, this study 
enhanced biomechanical understanding and practical 
applications in Criollo equestrian activities.
Comparison to other breeds
Comparison 
to 
published 
kinematic 
studies on other breeds underscores the specificity 
of Criollo’s gait dynamics. In the absence of kinetic 
or metabolic measurements, purely kinematic

2D Kinematic analysis of walk, trot, and canter gaits in Criollo breed horses.
Ciência Rural, v.56, n.3, 2026.
9
comparisons – based on stride length and duty factor 
(MARTINS et al., 2025; PIZZI et al., 2024; VERNA 
et al., 2022b) – underscore the unique, economical 
gait of Criollo horses across walk, trot and canter. 
In the current study, Criollo horses demonstrated 
stride characteristics indicative of an economical 
gait pattern that aligns with their use in endurance 
events, such as the Freio de Ouro. Such breed-
specific kinematic benchmarks allow for meaningful 
comparisons with other breeds’ gait parameters 
(BACK & CLAYTON, 2013). Future research 
could further enhance this comparative analysis by 
incorporating standardized measurements across 
multiple breeds under similar conditions to verify 
these kinematic distinctions.
Limitations of the study
Some limitations should be acknowledged 
to contextualize these findings. Firstly, the sample of 
horses included in this study may not fully represent 
the variability within the Criollo population, especially 
given the regional focus of the sample. Future studies 
involving a more diverse sampling of Criollo horses 
across broader geographical areas would further 
solidify these reference kinematic values.
Additionally, the assessment of soundness 
was based on a subjective evaluation rather than an 
objective lameness detection method. Implementing 
validated methods, such as inertial sensor-based 
lameness detection, could improve the accuracy of the 
initial health assessment and reduce potential biases 
from subjective observation. Another limitation 
involves the single-camera setup utilized, which 
restricts the analysis to a two-dimensional plane. As 
such, minor misalignments within the capture volume 
may have impacted the accuracy of certain kinematic 
variables. Employing multi-camera setups or three-
dimensional kinematic analysis could yield a more 
comprehensive understanding of these gait patterns 
and minimise parallax errors.
Furthermore, it is worth noting that while 
this study captured and quantified gait parameters 
effectively, it did not regulate horse velocity precisely 
across all trials. Kinematic variables are sensitive to 
speed, and while every effort was made to maintain 
a consistent gait, slight velocity variations may have 
influenced certain parameters. Future studies may 
benefit from controlled speed trials to further refine 
the accuracy of the kinematic data obtained.
Future directions
This 
study 
established 
foundational 
kinematic benchmarks for Criollo horses, offering 
key gait parameters essential for advancing 
biomechanical research and practical applications. 
Defining normal ranges for stride length, duty 
factor, and angular measurements, the study 
supports a detailed understanding of the breed’s 
movement profile, which is invaluable given 
the Criollo’s prominence in Brazilian equestrian 
activities alongside breeds like the Quarter Horse 
(MINISTÉRIO DA AGRICULTURA, 2016; DA 
SILVA & SILVA FARIAS, 2017). The kinematic data 
presented not only aid in characterising the Criollo 
gait but also underscore the need for breed- and 
discipline-specific research, crucial for investigating 
functional asymmetries and discerning pain-related 
gait changes (EGAN et al., 2019).
As technology advances, the ability to 
capture biomechanical data in natural, outdoor 
settings 
has 
become 
increasingly 
relevant, 
allowing researchers to simulate realistic training 
environments that complement laboratory findings 
(GALISTEO et al., 2010). Establishing kinematic 
values for Criollo horses represents an unprecedented 
contribution, supporting further studies on locomotor 
conditions, training optimisations, and performance 
assessments across diverse equestrian contexts. With 
emerging technologies enabling more sophisticated 
biomechanical and kinetic measurements, future 
work could integrate 3D analyses, metabolic cost 
assessments and longitudinal monitoring to deepen 
our understanding of Criollo performance and 
welfare. This study, however, establishes key 2D 
kinematic benchmarks for walk, trot and canter in 
clinically sound Criollo horses, providing a solid 
reference for intra-breed and longitudinal gait 
comparisons.
CONCLUSION
This study successfully characterized the 
kinematic variables of walk, trot, and canter gaits 
in Criollo horses, providing a crucial reference 
for future biomechanical research. Understanding 
these parameters is vital for optimising training, 
rehabilitation, and performance evaluation, benefiting 
both the scientific community and equestrian 
professionals.

in this study.
